questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue misses the clinical pattern: Acute Angle Glaucoma pain points to The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home misses the clinical pattern: Acute Angle Glaucoma pain points to The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well misses the clinical pattern: Acute Angle Glaucoma pain points to The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: Obtain a full allergy history before intervening misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Document the event and continue monitoring misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster misses the clinical pattern: C Diff Spore Precautions infection points to Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Place the client on droplet precautions only misses the clinical pattern: C Diff Spore Precautions infection points to Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel misses the clinical pattern: C Diff Spore Precautions infection points to Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: A client with diabetic ketoacidosis is lethargic, has dry mucous membranes, blood pressure 88/54 mmHg, and blood glucose 620 mg/dL. Which intervention should the nurse anticipate first?

options.text: Rapid isotonic fluid replacement.
Long-acting insulin by subcutaneous injection.
A potassium-restricted diet.
Sodium bicarbonate for all clients with DKA.

questions.rationale: The immediate threat in DKA is severe volume depletion and impaired perfusion. Isotonic fluid replacement is the first major intervention, followed by insulin and electrolyte management based on potassium status and response. Subcutaneous long-acting insulin and routine bicarbonate do not address the acute circulatory problem first.

distractorRationales.b: Long-acting insulin by subcutaneous injection misses the clinical pattern: Dka Fluid Priority perfusion points to Rapid isotonic fluid replacement, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: A potassium-restricted diet misses the clinical pattern: Dka Fluid Priority perfusion points to Rapid isotonic fluid replacement, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: Sodium bicarbonate for all clients with DKA misses the clinical pattern: Dka Fluid Priority perfusion points to Rapid isotonic fluid replacement, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: In DKA with hypotension and dehydration, restore circulating volume first.

questions.stem: A client using a fentanyl patch develops a fever of 102 F (38.9 C). Which instruction should the nurse provide?

options.text: Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption.
Place a heating pad over the patch to improve comfort.
Cut the patch in half if the client feels too sleepy.
Apply an extra patch if the pain feels worse.

questions.rationale: Heat and fever can increase fentanyl absorption from a transdermal patch and raise the risk of oversedation or respiratory depression. The client should report this promptly and avoid external heat sources. Cutting or doubling patches is unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: Place a heating pad over the patch to improve comfort misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Patch Fever Risk respiratory points to Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Cut the patch in half if the client feels too sleepy misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Patch Fever Risk respiratory points to Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Apply an extra patch if the pain feels worse misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Patch Fever Risk respiratory points to Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Transdermal fentanyl becomes riskier with fever or heat exposure because absorption can increase.

questions.stem: A client hospitalized with acute pancreatitis develops numbness around the mouth and carpal spasms. Which laboratory value does the nurse anticipate is most likely abnormal?

options.text: Low serum calcium.
High serum sodium.
Low hematocrit.
Low chloride.

questions.rationale: Perioral numbness and carpal spasm suggest neuromuscular irritability from hypocalcemia. Acute pancreatitis can lower serum calcium as part of the disease process. Recognizing the pattern helps the nurse connect clinical signs with the expected electrolyte disturbance.

distractorRationales.b: High serum sodium misses the clinical pattern: Pancreatitis Hypocalcemia Tetany electrolyte points to Low serum calcium, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Low hematocrit misses the clinical pattern: Pancreatitis Hypocalcemia Tetany electrolyte points to Low serum calcium, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Low chloride misses the clinical pattern: Pancreatitis Hypocalcemia Tetany electrolyte points to Low serum calcium, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Tetany-type symptoms in pancreatitis point toward hypocalcemia.

questions.stem: A client performing peritoneal dialysis reports cloudy outflow and abdominal pain. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis.
Tell the client cloudy drainage is expected after dialysis.
Instruct the client to skip the next exchange.
Have the client drink extra water and wait until tomorrow.

questions.rationale: Cloudy dialysate and abdominal pain are classic findings of peritoneal dialysis-associated peritonitis. The nurse should escalate promptly for assessment and treatment. Reassurance or delay can worsen infection and threaten the dialysis access pathway.

distractorRationales.b: Tell the client cloudy drainage is expected after dialysis misses the clinical pattern: Peritoneal Dialysis Peritonitis infection points to Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Instruct the client to skip the next exchange misses the clinical pattern: Peritoneal Dialysis Peritonitis infection points to Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Have the client drink extra water and wait until tomorrow misses the clinical pattern: Peritoneal Dialysis Peritonitis infection points to Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Cloudy peritoneal dialysis outflow should be treated as peritonitis until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A pregnant client at 32 weeks reports painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination.
Perform a sterile vaginal exam to assess dilation.
Instruct the client to walk in the hallway to check if the bleeding slows.
Prepare the client for a routine discharge if contractions are absent.

questions.rationale: Painless bright red bleeding in the third trimester suggests placenta previa. The nurse should assess maternal-fetal status while avoiding vaginal examination because digital exams can worsen hemorrhage when the placenta overlies the cervical os. Early recognition and protection from avoidable bleeding are the priority.

distractorRationales.b: Perform a sterile vaginal exam to assess dilation misses the clinical pattern: Placenta Previa Bleeding bleeding points to Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: Instruct the client to walk in the hallway to check if the bleeding slows misses the clinical pattern: Placenta Previa Bleeding bleeding points to Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Prepare the client for a routine discharge if contractions are absent misses the clinical pattern: Placenta Previa Bleeding bleeding points to Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Suspected placenta previa means monitor and protect; do not perform a vaginal exam.

questions.stem: A client 3 days after delivery calls the postpartum unit reporting a severe headache, visual spots, and swelling of the hands. Which instruction should the nurse give first?

options.text: Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation.
Take an over-the-counter pain reliever and rest in a dark room.
Limit fluids for the rest of the day.
Wait until the routine postpartum visit next week.

questions.rationale: Severe headache, visual changes, and edema after delivery raise concern for postpartum preeclampsia, which can progress to seizure or stroke. The client needs urgent evaluation rather than home symptom management. Delayed care can miss a serious hypertensive emergency.

distractorRationales.b: Take an over-the-counter pain reliever and rest in a dark room misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Preeclampsia Headache edema points to Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Limit fluids for the rest of the day misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Preeclampsia Headache edema points to Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Wait until the routine postpartum visit next week misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Preeclampsia Headache edema points to Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Severe headache and visual changes after delivery should be treated as postpartum preeclampsia until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A client is 4 hours postoperative after a thyroidectomy and develops inspiratory stridor. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare for emergency airway support and notify the provider immediately.
Offer ice chips to soothe the throat.
Document the finding as expected postoperative hoarseness.
Ask the client to speak loudly so the nurse can assess voice quality.

questions.rationale: Stridor after thyroid surgery may indicate airway obstruction from swelling, hematoma, or laryngeal nerve complications and requires immediate action. The nurse should treat it as an airway emergency rather than a routine sore-throat complaint. Delaying airway support can quickly become life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: Offer ice chips to soothe the throat misses the clinical pattern: Thyroidectomy Stridor airway points to Prepare for emergency airway support and notify the provider immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Document the finding as expected postoperative hoarseness misses the clinical pattern: Thyroidectomy Stridor airway points to Prepare for emergency airway support and notify the provider immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Ask the client to speak loudly so the nurse can assess voice quality misses the clinical pattern: Thyroidectomy Stridor airway points to Prepare for emergency airway support and notify the provider immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Post-thyroidectomy stridor is an airway emergency, not routine hoarseness.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient taking warfarin shows correct understanding?

options.text: "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away."
"I can double the dose if I miss one dose."
"I should stop the medication once I feel better."
"A green salad every day is unsafe no matter what."

questions.rationale: Warfarin can increase bleeding risk, so the patient should know to watch for and report bleeding signs. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Doubling a missed dose can be dangerous. - C Anticoagulants are not stopped casually. - D Vitamin K intake should be consistent, not completely avoided without guidance.

distractorRationales.b: "I can double the dose if I miss one dose." misses the clinical pattern: Warfarin Teaching bleeding points to "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away.", so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: "I should stop the medication once I feel better." misses the clinical pattern: Warfarin Teaching bleeding points to "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away.", so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: "A green salad every day is unsafe no matter what." misses the clinical pattern: Warfarin Teaching bleeding points to "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away.", so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Anticoagulant teaching should focus on bleeding safety and consistency.

questions.stem: After a patient falls while attempting to ambulate unassisted, the nurse completes an incident report. Which statement about incident reports is correct?

options.text: The incident report should be referenced in the patient's medical record to ensure continuity of care
The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical record and is not discoverable in litigation
Incident reports are part of the permanent medical record and must be provided to patients upon request
The nurse should document 'incident report filed' in the nursing notes to alert other providers

questions.rationale: Incident reports (occurrence reports) are internal quality improvement documents: they are NOT referenced in the patient's medical record. Referencing them in the chart (A, D) makes them discoverable in litigation, eliminating their protection as internal QI documents. The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without mentioning the incident report. In many states, incident reports prepared in anticipation of litigation may be protected from discovery, but only if not referenced in the chart. They are not part of the permanent medical record (C).

distractorRationales.a: The incident report should be referenced in the patient's medical record to ensure continuity of care misses the clinical pattern: Documentation assessment points to The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: Incident reports are part of the permanent medical record and must be provided to patients upon misses the clinical pattern: Documentation assessment points to The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: The nurse should document 'incident report filed' in the nursing notes to alert other providers misses the clinical pattern: Documentation assessment points to The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Incident reports: do NOT reference in the medical record; document clinical facts of the event in the chart only — referencing the incident report makes it legally discoverable.
